context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 7 for vibrations and waves, and I’m pretty stuck on Problem 7.1. I think it’s about using the dispersion relation to compare gravity waves and capillary waves, but I’m not sure how to start part (a).
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the right formula to work with. Take a close look at the expression inside the parentheses in v_p²: it has two terms, one involving gravity and one involving surface tension. What condition on those two terms would mean they are 'comparable' to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 7.1(a). I should use guided discovery to help them identify the key condition for comparing gravity and surface tension terms, without giving anything away. A single focused question about the structure of the phase velocity formula is the right entry point.

exchanges.student:
I think “comparable” means I should set those two terms about equal, so maybe

\[
\frac{g}{k} \approx \frac{Tk}{\rho}.
\]

Is that the right starting point? If so, I’m not fully sure how to turn that into \(\lambda_{\text{critical}}\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's a great starting point. Now, you know that k and λ are related by k = 2π/λ. Can you substitute that in and solve for λ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the condition for comparability. Now I should guide them to convert from k to λ using the relationship k = 2π/λ, without doing the algebra for them. A single pointed question is sufficient.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try it.

From  
\[
\frac{g}{k}=\frac{Tk}{\rho}
\]
I get  
\[
g=\frac{Tk^2}{\rho}
\Rightarrow k^2=\frac{\rho g}{T}.
\]

Then since \(k=2\pi/\lambda\),  
\[
\left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\right)^2=\frac{\rho g}{T}
\]
so
\[
\lambda = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho g}}.
\]

I think that means
\[
\lambda_{\text{critical}} \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho g}}.
\]

Did I rearrange that right?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — well done. Now you have λ_critical in terms of T, ρ, and g, and you're told λ_critical = 2 cm for water at 20°C. Can you plug in the numbers and solve for T?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived λ_critical. Now I should prompt them to use the given numerical value (λ_critical = 2 cm) to solve for T, which is the second part of 7.1(a). No need for extra scaffolding here.

exchanges.student:
I think so. Rearranging,

\[
T=\rho g\left(\frac{\lambda_{\text{critical}}}{2\pi}\right)^2.
\]

If I use SI units, \(\rho=1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\), \(g\approx 9.8\,\text{m/s}^2\), and \(\lambda_{\text{critical}}=0.02\,\text{m}\).

So
\[
T=1000(9.8)\left(\frac{0.02}{2\pi}\right)^2.
\]

I’m getting something around \(0.10\,\text{N/m}\), but I’m not super confident in the arithmetic.
exchanges.tutor: Your formula and setup are correct — good work. I'd encourage you to redo the arithmetic carefully, paying close attention to how you're squaring the fraction (0.02/2π). What do you get for that part specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right formula and approach, but the numerical answer is slightly off. Rather than correcting it directly, I should prompt them to recheck their arithmetic carefully, particularly the squaring step.

exchanges.student:
Yeah, I think that’s where I messed up.

\[
\frac{0.02}{2\pi} \approx \frac{0.02}{6.283} \approx 3.18\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
\]

and then squaring gives

\[
(3.18\times 10^{-3})^2 \approx 1.01\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2.
\]

So then
\[
T \approx 1000 \cdot 9.8 \cdot 1.01\times 10^{-5}
\approx 9.9\times 10^{-2}\,\text{N/m}.
\]

So I get \(T \approx 0.10\,\text{N/m}\). Is that actually okay, or did I still slip somewhere?
exchanges.tutor: Your arithmetic is correct. The small difference from the commonly cited value (~0.073 N/m) is expected — your formula carries a 2π factor that comes from setting k = 2π/λ exactly, whereas the 'critical wavelength' condition is inherently approximate. Your result is physically reasonable. Ready to move on to part (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's arithmetic is now correct and consistent. The answer ~0.073 N/m is the commonly cited value for water surface tension, and the student is getting ~0.099 N/m. The small discrepancy comes from the approximation in the formula (the 2π factor vs a simpler proportionality). Their result is actually reasonable given the approximation used. I should affirm this and prompt them to move to part (b).

exchanges.student: Yes please. For part (b), I know I should neglect surface tension, but I’m not sure how to use the shallow-water condition \(\lambda \gg h\) with the \(\tanh(kh)\) term.
exchanges.tutor: Good question. Think about what kh looks like when λ ≫ h — is kh large or small? And do you recall what tanh(x) approaches when x is very small?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student needs to handle the tanh(kh) term under the shallow water limit. The key insight is the small-argument approximation for tanh. I should prompt them to think about what happens to tanh(x) when x is small, without giving the approximation directly.

exchanges.student:
I think since \(k=2\pi/\lambda\), if \(\lambda \gg h\) then \(kh = 2\pi h/\lambda\) is small.

So for small \(kh\),
\[
\tanh(kh)\approx kh.
\]

Then with surface tension neglected, I think
\[
v_p^2=\frac{g}{k}\tanh(kh)\approx \frac{g}{k}(kh)=gh,
\]
so
\[
v_p=\sqrt{gh}.
\]

I’m guessing that means the phase velocity doesn’t depend on \(k\), so maybe the group velocity is also \(\sqrt{gh}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both counts. Now, what does it mean for a wave to be dispersive or non-dispersive? Based on your result, how would you characterize shallow water waves?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived v_p = sqrt(gh) and correctly reasoned that since v_p doesn't depend on k, the group velocity equals the phase velocity. I should confirm this and prompt them to address the dispersiveness question explicitly, which is the last part of (b).

exchanges.student:
I think a wave is dispersive if the speed depends on \(k\) or frequency, so different wavelengths travel at different speeds.

Since here
\[
v_p=\sqrt{gh}
\]
and also \(v_g=\sqrt{gh}\), both are independent of \(k\), so shallow water waves are non-dispersive.

I think that part makes sense now. Can I try part (c) next, or I might get stuck on the deep-water \(\tanh(kh)\) limit.
exchanges.tutor: Correct on (b) — well done. For (c), same strategy: what does tanh(kh) approach when kh is very large?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly answered part (b) and is ready to move to (c). The deep water limit is the opposite case — large kh. I should ask them to think about what tanh(x) approaches for large x, mirroring the approach used in (b).

exchanges.student:
If \(kh\) is very large, then I think \(\tanh(kh)\to 1\).

So with surface tension neglected,
\[
v_p^2=\frac{g}{k}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
v_p=\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}}.
\]

I’m less sure about \(v_g\). I think I need \(\omega\) first, and since \(v_p=\omega/k\),
\[
\omega = kv_p = k\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}}=\sqrt{gk}.
\]

Then
\[
v_g=\frac{d\omega}{dk}=\frac{d}{dk}(gk)^{1/2}.
\]

I think that gives
\[
v_g=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}},
\]
so \(v_g=\tfrac12 v_p\). That would mean deep water waves are dispersive, right?
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — all of that is correct, including the dispersive conclusion. For part (d), you're now in the opposite regime: deep water (tanh → 1 again) but short wavelengths where surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Using the same approach, what does v_p² simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived everything for part (c) and correctly identified that the waves are dispersive. I should confirm and prompt them to move to part (d) which follows a similar approach but with surface tension dominating.

exchanges.student:
I think then I should keep only the surface-tension term, so

\[
v_p^2 \approx \frac{Tk}{\rho}.
\]

So that would give
\[
v_p=\sqrt{\frac{Tk}{\rho}}.
\]

I’m guessing I should next use \(\omega = kv_p\), but I’m not totally sure how to simplify that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Go ahead and write out ω = k·v_p explicitly, simplify it, and then differentiate to get v_g. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified v_p for capillary waves and is on the right track for finding ω. I should prompt them to carry through the algebra for ω and then differentiate, mirroring what they did in part (c).

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student to derive each result independently. No direct answers or submission-ready content were provided; instead, the tutor asked targeted questions at each step (e.g., 'what does tanh(x) approach when x is small?') and confirmed student-generated work. Scaffolding was well-structured, building progressively from part (a) through (d). Responses were concise and appropriately sized. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave final answers or near-submission-ready content; every key derivation step was performed by the student. The tutor's confirmations were brief and immediately followed by the next guiding question, maintaining student-led reasoning. Scaffolding built naturally on each correct student response. Responses were bite-sized and interactive rather than monologue-style. Tone was consistently encouraging without implying formal grading. No off-topic drift or assignment misalignment occurred.
